phenomenon: Neutron Star Mergers and Kilonovae -- r-process Nucleosynthesis
key_measurement: GW170817 produced approximately 16,000 Earth masses of r-process heavy elements, including approximately 10 Earth masses of gold and platinum combined; gravitational wave chirp mass measured at 1.188 +0.004/-0.002 solar masses; source at 40 +8/-8 Mpc (approximately 130 million light-years) in galaxy NGC 4993 (Abbott et al. 2017, ApJL 848 L12; arXiv:1710.05833)
principle_set: GW170817 (17 August 2017, 12:41:04.4 UTC) was the first gravitational wave event definitively correlated with electromagnetic observations -- the first multi-messenger astronomy event -- detected by LIGO (Hanford and Livingston) and Virgo interferometers in the galaxy NGC 4993 approximately 140 million light-years away

The merger of two neutron stars produced short gamma-ray burst GRB 170817A, detected by Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope 1.74 +/- 0.05 seconds after the merger signal and independently confirmed by INTEGRAL; this coincidence proved gravitational waves travel at the speed of light to within 3x10^-15 times c

Eleven hours after the merger, optical transient AT 2017gfo (SSS17a) was detected by the Swope Telescope at Las Campanas Observatory in NGC 4993 -- this was the kilonova: an isotropically expanding fireball of radioactive r-process nuclei ejected at approximately 10% of light speed and glowing from radioactive decay heat

The kilonova spectrum evolved from blue to red over approximately 10 days as ejecta expanded and cooled -- spectral modeling identified r-process elements strontium and yttrium in the AT 2017gfo ejecta, providing the first direct spectroscopic confirmation that neutron star mergers are sites of r-process nucleosynthesis (Watson et al. 2019, Nature 574, 497)

The r-process (rapid neutron capture) occurs in extreme neutron-rich environments: free neutrons are captured by seed nuclei faster than beta-decay can occur, building up elements far beyond iron including gold (Z=79), platinum (Z=78), uranium (Z=92), and the lanthanides

GW170817 confirmed neutron star mergers as a primary site for r-process nucleosynthesis -- accounting for roughly half of all elements heavier than iron in the universe; approximately 16,000 Earth masses of heavy elements were produced in this single event including approximately 10 Earth masses just of gold and platinum

The multi-messenger paper (arXiv:1710.05833) is authored by approximately 4,000 astronomers from over 900 institutions -- roughly one-third of the worldwide astronomical community -- with observations from 70+ observatories across all 7 continents and in space; awarded Science magazine Breakthrough of the Year 2017
framework: Multi-messenger astronomy; r-process nucleosynthesis theory; kilonova light curve models (Metzger et al. 2010); neutron star merger astrophysics (Li & Paczynski 1998)
institutions: LIGO Scientific Collaboration (Caltech, MIT, and partner institutions)
NASA Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope team (Goddard Space Flight Center)
Carnegie Observatories -- One-Meter Two Hemisphere (1M2H) team (Swope Telescope discovery)
University of Copenhagen (Watson et al. -- strontium identification)

sources.claim: GW170817 multi-messenger observations: gravitational wave + GRB 170817A + kilonova AT 2017gfo; approximately 16,000 Earth masses of r-process elements produced including ~10 Earth masses of gold and platinum; authored by ~4,000 astronomers from 900+ institutions
sources.citation: Abbott et al. (LIGO, Virgo and collaborations) 2017, ApJL 848, L12 -- Multi-messenger Observations of a Binary Neutron Star Merger

sources.claim: Gravitational wave detection of binary neutron star inspiral GW170817; component masses 0.86-2.26 solar masses; chirp mass 1.188 solar masses; distance 40 +8/-8 Mpc; Fermi detected GRB 170817A 1.74 +/- 0.05 s after merger
sources.citation: Abbott et al. (LIGO Scientific Collaboration & Virgo Collaboration) 2017, Physical Review Letters 119, 161101

sources.claim: First spectroscopic identification of r-process element strontium in kilonova AT 2017gfo ejecta from GW170817, providing direct proof that neutron star mergers are r-process nucleosynthesis sites

Kilonova predicted as electromagnetic counterpart to neutron star mergers powered by radioactive decay of r-process nuclei; peak brightness approximately 1000x classical nova -- coining of the term kilonova
GRB 170817A jointly detected by Fermi and INTEGRAL; gravitational wave and gamma-ray signals jointly constrain difference in speed of gravity and light to between -3x10^-15 and +7x10^-16 times c
sources.citation: Abbott et al. 2017, ApJL 848, L13 -- Gravitational Waves and Gamma-Rays from a Binary Neutron Star Merger

sources.claim: JWST observations of kilonova GRB 230307A confirmed heavy element production (tellurium and lanthanides) in a compact binary merger, extending the GW170817 result to a second event

visualization_notes: This subject offers the most cinematically powerful imagery in the series -- the literal forging of gold in space. Primary visual arc: two dense neutron stars (depicted as perfectly spherical, city-sized objects ~10 km diameter) orbiting each other in an accelerating death spiral. As they approach, space-time warps visibly around them -- gravitational wave ripples radiating outward like rings on the fabric of space. The inspiral lasts ~100 seconds of audible chirp signal -- this can be sonified using the publicly released LIGO audio. At the moment of merger: a blinding flash -- the short gamma-ray burst -- jets of energy lancing outward along the rotation axis. Then expanding around the collision site: the kilonova. Early phase (first 48 hours): a brilliant blue-white fireball expanding at 10% the speed of light, hot and rich in light r-process elements. Over 10 days: the fireball reddens and dims as heavier lanthanide-rich ejecta dominate -- the red color from opacity of heavy nuclei absorbing blue light. Scale context: this single explosion created 10 Earth masses of gold and platinum. Pan to show NGC 4993 galaxy at 40 Mpc -- the event a tiny blue-to-red spark in one elliptical galaxy. Available public images: NASA/ESA Hubble image of AT 2017gfo in NGC 4993 (publicly released October 16, 2017; credit NASA/ESA/STScI); Chandra X-ray image of GW170817 afterglow; ESO artist impressions (Creative Commons); LIGO chirp audio (publicly released). Most cinematically powerful moment: the color transition from blue to red kilonova over 10 days -- a time-lapse in which the screen literally turns from blue-white to red-gold as heavy nuclei form.
visualization_compliance: All GW170817 imagery is public-domain or under NASA/ESA open-access policy -- credit NASA/ESA/STScI for Hubble images
